findings.notes: Three db.exec calls use raw SQL DDL including CREATE TRIGGER with BEFORE UPDATE/DELETE and RAISE(ABORT). Turso lacks CREATE TRIGGER support; append-only enforcement must move to application layer.

findings.signature: CREATE INDEX ... ON mutation_ledger(CAST(json_extract(linked_memory_ids, '$[0]') AS INTEGER), mutation_type, timestamp)
findings.notes: Index expression uses json_extract and CAST inside CREATE INDEX. Turso does not support json_extract in index expressions; must denormalize or drop this index.

findings.signature: CREATE TRIGGER prevent_ledger_update BEFORE UPDATE ON mutation_ledger BEGIN SELECT RAISE(ABORT, ...) END
findings.notes: BEFORE UPDATE and BEFORE DELETE triggers with RAISE(ABORT) enforce append-only. Turso has no trigger support; immutability must be enforced in application code.

findings.signature: INSERT INTO mutation_ledger (mutation_type, reason, prior_hash, new_hash, linked_memory_ids, decision_meta, actor, session_id) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?)
findings.notes: Standard INSERT; lastInsertRowid cast to number. Compatible with Turso but must verify BigInt handling differs from better-sqlite3.

findings.signature: SELECT ml.* FROM mutation_ledger ml, json_each(ml.linked_memory_ids) je WHERE je.value = ?
findings.notes: Uses json_each() table-valued function to expand JSON arrays in JOIN. Turso does not support json_each; must denormalize linked_memory_ids into a junction table.

findings.signature: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM mutation_ledger WHERE json_extract(decision_meta, '$.normalizedPath') = ?
findings.notes: WHERE clause uses json_extract on decision_meta column. Turso lacks json_extract; must extract normalizedPath into a dedicated column.

findings.signature: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type = 'trigger' AND tbl_name = 'mutation_ledger'
findings.notes: Queries sqlite_master for trigger existence. Turso sqlite_master may not list triggers if unsupported; verification logic needs rework.
Wraps read-then-append in runInTransaction. Turso transactions work differently (HTTP-based); retry logic must adapt.

findings.signature: UPDATE memory_index SET file_mtime_ms = ? WHERE canonical_file_path = ? OR file_path = ?
findings.notes: Standard UPDATE with OR predicate. Compatible with Turso; verify statement preparation API matches.

findings.signature: UPDATE memory_index SET file_path=?, canonical_file_path=?, spec_folder=?, file_mtime_ms=?, updated_at=datetime('now') WHERE id=? AND embedding_status != 'failed'
findings.notes: Uses datetime('now') in SET clause and prepared statement .run(). Compatible with Turso datetime(); verify lastInsertRowid behavior.
Uses better-sqlite3 synchronous db.transaction() wrapper. Turso uses async transactions over HTTP; must convert to async.
PRAGMA table_info for schema introspection. Turso/libSQL may return different PRAGMA result shapes; verify compatibility.

findings.signature: INSERT INTO memory_idempotency_receipts (...) VALUES (...) ON CONFLICT(receipt_key) DO UPDATE SET response_payload=excluded.response_payload, memory_id=excluded.memory_id, updated_at=datetime('now')
findings.notes: INSERT ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE (upsert) with datetime('now'). Turso supports upsert; verify .run() return shape matches better-sqlite3.

findings.signature: UPDATE memory_index SET near_duplicate_of = NULL, last_dedup_checked_at = NULL WHERE id = ?
findings.notes: Standard UPDATE with NULL assignments. Compatible with Turso; low migration friction.

findings.signature: UPDATE memory_index SET near_duplicate_of = ?, last_dedup_checked_at = datetime('now') WHERE id = ?
findings.notes: UPDATE with datetime('now') and JSON.stringify of hint. Compatible with Turso; low migration friction.

findings.signature: SELECT source_id, target_id, relation FROM causal_edges WHERE relation IN ('contradicts','supersedes') AND source_id IN (?) AND target_id IN (?)
findings.notes: Read-only query on causal_edges with IN-list and string interpolation for placeholders. Compatible with Turso; uses prepared statement.

findings.signature: async applyStatediffActions(actions, sinks) → await sink.apply(action) for each ordered action
findings.notes: Async sink-based write dispatch. Already async-native; Turso async transaction model fits naturally. Sinks own the actual SQL.
